by
  rw [identDistrib_iff_hasSameLaws]
  have h₁ : HasLaw (fun ω ↦ e₁ ∈ (G ω).edgeSet) ({true, false} : Set (Bool)) P := by
    apply hasLaw_of_le
    -- The function (fun ω => e₁ ∈ (G ω).edgeSet) is Ω → Set.Iic ⊤, which is trivially measurable
    apply Measurable.congr (measurable_const)
    <;> simp_all [hasLaw_iff]
    <;> try aesop
    <;> try omega
  have h₂ : HasLaw (fun ω ↦ e₂ ∈ (G ω).edgeSet) ({true, false} : Set (Bool)) P := by
    apply hasLaw_of_le
    -- The function (fun ω => e₂ ∈ (G ω).edgeSet) is Ω → Set.Iic ⊤, which is trivially measurable
    apply Measurable.congr (measurable_const)
    <;> simp_all [hasLaw_iff]
    <;> try aesop
    <;> try omega
  -- Since both functions have the same distribution, their laws are the same
  have h₃ : HasSameLaws (fun ω ↦ e₁ ∈ (G ω).edgeSet) (fun ω ↦ e₂ ∈ (G ω).edgeSet) P := by
    have h₄ : HasLaw (fun ω ↦ e₁ ∈ (G ω).edgeSet) ({true, false} : Set (Bool)) P := h₁
    have h₅ : HasLaw (fun ω ↦ e₂ ∈ (G ω).edgeSet) ({true, false} : Set (Bool)) P := h₂
    -- Use the fact that both functions have the same distribution to conclude they have the same laws
    have h₆ : HasSameLaws (fun ω ↦ e₁ ∈ (G ω).edgeSet) (fun ω ↦ e₂ ∈ (G ω).edgeSet) P := by
      have h₇ := hG.getdistribution
      simp_all [hasLaw_iff]
      <;> try aesop
      <;> try omega
    exact h₆
  exact h₃

    have h₄ : ∀ (e : Sym2 V), e ∉ diagSet → IdentDistrib (fun S : Set (Sym2 V) => e₁ ∈ S) (fun S : Set (Sym2 V) => e ∈ S) (G(V, p)) (G(V, p)) := by
      intro e he
      have h₅ : IsBernoulliOn edgeSet diagSetᶜ p G(V, p) := by
        simpa [SimpleGraph.IsBinomialRandom] using hG
      -- Use the Bernoulli property to show that the distributions are the same

      exact h₆
    have h₅ : e₁ ∉ diagSet := by
      simp [Sym2.diagSet]

    have h₆ : IdentDistrib (fun ω => e₁ ∈ (G ω).edgeSet) (fun ω => e₁ ∈ (G ω).edgeSet) P P := by

    have h₇ : IdentDistrib (fun ω => e₁ ∈ (G ω).edgeSet) (fun ω => e₂ ∈ (G ω).edgeSet) P P := by

fun ω => e₁ ∈ (G ω).edgeSet` and `g := fun ω => e₂ ∈ (G ω).edgeSet` are `Ω → Prop`. Thus, for any subset `A ⊆ Prop`, `P (f ∈ A) = P (g ∈ A)` is required.

But for any `f : Ω → Prop`, `f ∈ A` means that the proposition `f ω` is in `A`. 

However, for a proposition `P`, the only subsets of `Prop` that can matter are `∅`, `{true}`, `{false}`, and `{true, false}` because every other subset is equivalent to one of these under propositions. 

But perhaps better: if `f` is a proposition-valued function, then `P (f ∈ A)` is `P (f)`, because `A` is either `∅` (measure 0), `{true}` (measure `P (f)`), `{false}` (measure `P (¬f)`), etc. 

But we are in trouble here because the statement as given is not generally true unless `e₁.IsDiag = e₂.IsDiag`, because if `e₁.IsDiag` and `e₂` is not, then `P (e₁ ∈ G.EdgeSet) = 0`, `P (e₂ ∈ G.EdgeSet) = p`, and if `p ≠ 0`, they are not identically distributed. 

But perhaps Lean's `IdentDistrib` is not what we think it is.
